Manufacturing should-cost intelligence

Know what a part should cost — before you negotiate.

CostVision breaks any part down to a defensible piece price across casting, sheet metal, machining, plastics, forging and PCB — from a photo, a CAD file, or a plain-English brief.

CV

CostVision — Help Centre

V4.1

Everything you need to master AI-powered should-costing

🔍
1
Navigate to a Commodity

There are two ways to start a costing:

  • New Costing (top bar, or press ⌘K) → opens the full commodity picker screen with all 19+ manufacturing processes and specialist tools as tiles → click a tile to open the right-side workflow panel.
  • One-Click Shortcuts on the Home Dashboard → jump directly to a specific commodity in full-screen mode.
  • 🚗 Automotive Software Should-Cost — new in V4.1 — select from the picker to open the dedicated 49-module software cost model for premium EV programmes.

Use Home in the left sidebar or click the CostVision logo to return to the dashboard at any time.

2
Enter Part Data
  • Fill in the Part Name at the top of the form.
  • Enter material, operations, and process-specific parameters.
  • Set Volume, Overhead %, and Margin % in Universal Costs.
  • Optionally upload a part photo — it appears on the PDF report.
3
Select Region & Currency

Use the Region selector in the top bar to switch manufacturing location. All machine rates, labour rates, and material prices adjust automatically for the selected country. Use the Currency dropdown to display costs in your preferred currency.

4
Click Calculate

Press Calculate to run the 8-bucket cost model: Raw Material → Process → Labour → Tooling → Packaging → Logistics → Overhead → Margin. Results appear in the right panel with interactive charts, sensitivity analysis, and AI-powered insights.

5
Export & Save
  • Export Excel — 6-sheet workbook with full cost breakdown and rate buildup.
  • PDF Report — 7-section professional report with AI insights.
  • Save Scenario — store configurations and compare multiple designs side-by-side.
▶
1:32
Video Tutorial
CostVision Full Walkthrough
New Costing → Enter data → Calculate → Export PDF in under 2 minutes
✦
What is the AI Agent?

The AI Agent tab uses Claude (Anthropic) to understand natural language descriptions of your part and automatically populate the cost model. Simply describe what you need costed — the AI handles the rest.

→
How to Use the AI Agent
  1. Click the AI Agent ✦ tab.
  2. Type a description, e.g.: "Aluminium 6061 bracket, 0.8kg, CNC milled, 3 operations, 50k annual volume, UK manufacture"
  3. Optionally upload a part photo or CAD file.
  4. The AI will auto-fill material, machine selections, cycle times, and volumes.
  5. Review the suggestions, adjust if needed, then click Calculate.
⚠
AI Validation

CostVision validates all AI-returned IDs against the rate library. If the AI suggests a material or machine that doesn't exist in the library, it will be flagged with a warning toast and nulled out — you'll need to select manually.

⚡
Tips for Better Results
  • Mention specific alloys: "Al 6082-T6", "Grade 304 stainless"
  • Include annual volume for accurate tooling amortisation
  • Specify the manufacturing country for regional rate accuracy
  • Add surface finish requirements (anodising, powder coat, painting)
💬
AI Cost Assistant

The floating chat button (bottom-right of any screen) opens the AI Cost Assistant — a real-time chatbot trained for manufacturing cost engineering. Ask natural-language questions such as:

  • "What is the should-cost for a HPDC aluminium housing?"
  • "How do I estimate machining cycle time?"
  • "What drives PCB fabrication cost?"
  • "Tooling amortisation for 50k volume — casting vs. injection?"

The assistant returns structured, numbered step-by-step guidance with cost drivers, DFM checks, and supplier negotiation tips. Responses are kept concise (<200 words) for quick reference.

▶
2:14
Video Tutorial
AI Agent in Action
Describe a part in plain English → AI fills the full cost model automatically
🎬 Interactive Demo — PCB Image-to-Cost

A self-running, narrated walkthrough of turning a PCB photo into a full costed Bill of Materials — built for new team members. Press ▶ Play for the auto-narrated tour, or step through with Next / Prev. Each scene explains what to do and why it matters.

Intro
CostVision · Product Demo
1 / 11
📁
Supported File Types
  • Photos: JPG, PNG, HEIC — uploaded via the Part Photo zone (always visible)
  • CAD for AI analysis: PNG/JPG screenshots of STEP, DXF, or SolidWorks files via the AI CAD ✦ tab
🖼
Part Photo

The part photo appears on the PDF report cover page. Drag and drop an image onto the upload zone, or click to browse. Maximum recommended size: 5 MB. Photos are stored in-browser only — never uploaded to any server.

🤖
AI CAD Analysis

Select the AI CAD ✦ tab and upload a screenshot or rendering of your part. The AI will detect features (holes, pockets, walls, ribs), estimate complexity, suggest manufacturing processes, and auto-fill relevant cost model parameters. A DfM Score and Process Confidence rating are shown.

🎬 Demo Script & Timeline (for a screen-recorded walkthrough)

Use the interactive player above to learn the feature. To record a polished screen-capture video for the team, here is the ready-to-read narration script with on-screen actions and timings — a ~2-minute runtime.

TimeOn screen — what to doSay this (narration)
0:00–0:12Dashboard. Have a clear PCB photo ready."This is PCB Image-to-Cost. I'll turn a single photo of a circuit board into a full, priced Bill of Materials — no manual entry."
0:12–0:25Open the PCB Fab 🔬 (or PCBA 🔬) tab."Open PCB Fab for the bare board, or PCBA for the assembled board. Both have the Image-to-BOM upload zone."
0:25–0:45Drag the photo onto the 🔬 zone. Mention adding top/bottom/angle + a ruler."Drag the photo in. For best accuracy, add top, bottom and an angled view, plus a ruler or coin so the board can be measured."
0:45–1:05Watch the progress bar through the four stages."The AI classifies the board, reads the markings, extracts every component, then prices it — a few seconds."
1:05–1:25Point at the board spec (size/layers/finish) and the BOM table."Here's the board — 80 by 60 mil, four layers, ENIG — and the full BOM: every part with its number, package and quantity."
1:25–1:45Hover a ⚠️ flagged line and a ✅ catalogue-verified line."Every line is tagged: catalogue-verified prices are real distributor data; low-confidence lines are flagged so you know exactly what to check. Same photo always gives the same result."
1:45–2:05Show fab + assembly cost, country compare, click Export."And the full should-cost — bare board, assembly, per-board total — with a country comparison and one-click export. A costed BOM from a photo, in about two minutes."

Recording tips: 1080p, capture just the browser window. Use a sharp, evenly-lit board photo (glare hides markings). Record narration separately for clean audio. Pause ~1s on the BOM table and on a flagged line so viewers can read them.

Machining

CNC turning, milling, grinding. Enter material, operations with machine/labour IDs, cycle times, and OEE.

Sheet Metal

Press shop stamping — blanking + forming dies. Progressive or transfer tooling options.

SM Fabrication

Fabrication shop: Laser Cut / Turret / Shear → Press Brake → optional welding. Process advisor included.

Injection Moulding

Thermoplastics. Includes runner system, tolerance and surface finish multipliers, multi-cavity tooling.

Casting

HPDC, sand, gravity, investment casting. Reject rate and yield modelling. As-cast parts only.

Cast+Machine

Combined casting + post-machining operations. Heat treatment and impregnation included.

Forging

Open die, closed die, and near-net-shape forging. Flash and trim allowance modelling.

PCBA / SMD

PCB assembly: SMT placement, reflow, wave solder, conformal coat, NRE. CPH-based line modelling.

Composites

CFRP/GFRP: hand layup, prepreg, RTM, VARTM, filament winding. Autoclave/press cure cycles.

Wiring Harness

Wire gauges, connectors, splices, conduit, tape, crimping assembly, electrical test included.

Rubber

Compression, transfer, LSR injection moulding, extrusion+vulcanise. EPDM, NBR, Silicone, FKM.

Assembly BOM

Full BOM costing: upload or enter component list with costs, then add assembly operations on top.

🚗 Automotive SW Should-Cost V4.1 NEW

Parameter-driven software cost model for premium luxury EV programmes. 49 modules × 7 categories, ASIL QM–D multipliers, 9 regional rates, complexity/reuse factors, sensitivity analysis, and OEM benchmark comparisons.

🚗 Automotive Software Should-Cost — Complete Beginner's Guide

This tool helps you estimate how much it costs to develop all the software inside a modern electric vehicle. Think of it as a detailed price calculator — but instead of shopping items, you are pricing out engineering teams, safety certifications, tools, and licences for 49 separate software systems.

You do not need to be a software engineer to use this. Just follow the steps below one at a time.

49 Software Modules 7 Categories 5 Vehicle Demos ISO 26262 ASIL Safety Benchmark & Deep-Dive Reports

🆕 Latest update (July 2026): now 49 modules (43 core + 6 premium-trim options — active body control, premium audio/ANC, automated parking, climate, digital key, AR-HUD), one-click Demo Programmes for five real cars (Range Rover L460, BMW X7, Audi Q8, Mercedes GLS, Porsche Cayenne) across ICE/MHEV/PHEV/BEV, and four exportable comparison reports. Back-tested to a ±25–35% envelope (MAPE 24.9% vs 7 published programmes).

🎬 Interactive Demo — Watch How It Works

A self-running, narrated walkthrough of the whole feature — built for new team members. Press ▶ Play and it advances automatically with narration, or step through it yourself with Next / Prev. Every scene explains not just what to click but why it matters.

Intro
CostVision · Product Demo
1 / 13
💡
Background: Why Does Car Software Cost So Much?

A modern electric vehicle contains 100–150 million lines of code — more than a commercial aircraft. Unlike a physical part that you can stamp out in a factory, software costs come almost entirely from human engineering time. Here is where the money goes:

👨‍💻 Development (biggest chunk)
Software engineers writing, reviewing, and integrating code. A senior embedded engineer costs £80–120k/year. A full EV software team can be 500–2,000 people over 5–7 years.
🧪 Testing & Validation
Safety-critical software must be tested exhaustively. Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) rigs, test automation, and formal verification can cost as much as development itself.
🛠 Tools & Licences
Development tools (MATLAB/Simulink, Vector CANalyzer, LDRA) cost £50k–£200k per year per team. Third-party IP licences (maps, voice AI, RTOS royalties) add further cost.
🔒 Safety Certification (ASIL)
ISO 26262 mandates rigorous processes for safety-critical software. Achieving ASIL-D (highest safety) requires 3× more documentation, reviews, and independent audits than non-safety software.

Real-world scale: BMW spent ~£620M developing software for the iX. Tesla spent ~£850M on HW4. This tool lets you build your own estimate from first principles and compare it to those benchmarks.

1
How to Open the Auto SW Cost Tool
  1. Click the New Costing button in the top bar (or press ⌘K).
  2. The Start New Should Costing screen appears, showing tiles for all available commodity types.
  3. Scroll down to the Software section.
  4. Click the 💻 Auto SW Cost tile.
  5. The Automotive Software Should-Cost panel opens on the right side of the screen.

Tip: You can also click 🚗 Auto SW Cost directly from the Home dashboard if you see that shortcut tile there.

2
Step 2 — Fill In Programme Parameters

The first section is called Programme Parameters. These are the top-level settings that describe the overall vehicle programme. They affect every module's cost.

Programme Name — Give your project a name (e.g. "Project Alpine EV"). This is just a label for your reference and appears on exports.
Programme Life (years) — How many years will this software be developed and maintained? Typical EV programmes last 5–8 years from first concept to end-of-production. Why it matters: tool licences, cloud costs, and maintenance are all charged per year, so a longer programme means higher totals.
Annual Production Volume — How many vehicles per year will this software go into? (e.g. 50,000 vehicles/year). Why it matters: per-vehicle costs like royalties (paying a licence fee every time a car is sold) are divided across this number. Higher volume = lower cost per vehicle.
Global Labour Region — Where are your engineers located? This sets the base salary rate for the entire programme. Options include UK, EU, USA Silicon Valley, China, India, Eastern Europe, Japan, and Mexico. Why it matters: an engineer in Silicon Valley costs ~9× more than an engineer in India for the same work.
Global Overhead % and Margin % — Overhead covers office space, HR, IT infrastructure, and management. Margin is the profit the supplier adds on top. Typical values: Overhead 25–35%, Margin 8–15%. Leave these at defaults if unsure.
3
Step 3 — Start Fast: Demo Programmes or a Preset
🚗 Demo Programmes (the fastest start)

In the Demo Programmes row, one click loads a fully-configured real vehicle and calculates instantly — the best way to see a credible result before you change anything.

🇬🇧 Range Rover L460 (PHEV) 🇩🇪 BMW X7 (48V MHEV) 🇩🇪 Audi Q8 (MHEV) 🇩🇪 Mercedes GLS (MHEV) 🇩🇪 Porsche Cayenne (ICE / MHEV / PHEV / Electric)

Each demo also has a View full report button (detailed board-level breakdown). See Step 6 for the report types.

Prefer to build from scratch? Click one of the Quick-Set Presets. A preset automatically fills in all 49 modules with sensible values, so you can see results immediately and then adjust individual modules afterwards.

🚀 Aggressive — Sets the lowest possible costs: minimal safety requirements (ASIL-A), maximum reuse of existing code, and cheap offshore labour (China rates). Use this to see the floor — the absolute minimum spend if everything goes perfectly.
📊 Industry Baseline — Sets typical real-world values used by a Tier-1 supplier. Module-default ASIL levels, medium code reuse, UK labour rates. Start here if unsure — this is the most realistic starting point.
👑 Premium OEM — Sets the highest costs: maximum complexity, all-new (greenfield) development, Silicon Valley labour rates. Use this to see the ceiling — what a top-tier programme like Tesla or BMW actually spends.
🌏 Offshored — Default ASIL and complexity but with India labour rates. Represents an offshore-first development strategy common in cost-reduction programmes.

Recommended first-time flow: Click 📊 Industry Baseline → scroll down → click Calculate → review results → come back and tweak individual modules.

4
Step 4 — Configure Individual Modules (The Module Table)

Below the presets, you will see a table of all 49 software modules grouped into 7 categories. Each row is one software system in the vehicle. You can adjust three settings per module:

43 core + 6 premium. 43 modules are on by default. Six premium-trim options — active body control, premium audio & active noise control, automated parking, climate control, digital key (UWB), and AR head-up display — default to off (a base car may not have them). Tick them on for a flagship programme; the Demo Programmes enable the right ones automatically.

🔴 ASIL Level — "How safe does this software need to be?"

ASIL stands for Automotive Safety Integrity Level. It comes from the global standard ISO 26262 for road vehicle safety. The higher the ASIL, the more rigorous (and expensive) the development process must be.

QM
No safety req.
1.00×
ASIL-A
Low hazard
1.35×
ASIL-B
Medium hazard
1.80×
ASIL-C
High hazard
2.30×
ASIL-D
Max hazard
3.20×

Examples: The infotainment screen (IVI) is QM — if it crashes, nobody gets hurt. The AEB braking system is ASIL-D — a failure could kill someone, so it costs 3.2× more to develop. The tool pre-fills each module with its typical real-world ASIL level. Only change these if you have a specific engineering reason to.

⚠ Warning badges: If you assign a lower ASIL than the module's engineering default (e.g. setting ASIL-A on a braking system that should be ASIL-D), the tool shows an orange warning badge. This is not blocked — you can still calculate — but it flags an unsafe assumption.

🟡 Complexity — "How hard is this software to build?"

Complexity reflects how algorithmically difficult, novel, or large the software is. It multiplies the base development cost for that module.

  • Low (0.60×) — Simple, well-understood software. Example: a basic gateway ECU with fixed routing rules.
  • Medium (1.00×) — Standard complexity. Most infotainment, body control, and diagnostics modules.
  • High (1.70×) — Complex algorithms or large-scale integration. Example: ADAS sensor fusion combining camera + radar + lidar.
  • Very High (2.80×) — Frontier engineering. Example: real-time AI perception for autonomous driving.
🟢 Reuse — "Are you building from scratch or copying from a previous car?"

Reuse is the biggest single cost lever in automotive software. If your company already has working software from a previous vehicle, you do not need to rewrite it — you just adapt it. This dramatically reduces development cost.

  • Fresh / Greenfield (1.00×) — Written from scratch. No prior code to build on. Maximum cost.
  • Light reuse (0.82×) — A previous version exists but needs significant rework (e.g. porting to a new microprocessor).
  • Medium reuse (0.60×) — Core algorithms reused, new platform integration required. Common on facelift programmes.
  • Heavy reuse (0.35×) — Mostly copy-paste with minor changes. Very low development effort.
  • Platform reuse (0.14×) — Shared across multiple vehicle lines with almost no changes. Example: AUTOSAR stack shared across 10 models.

Which modules should I change? Focus on the modules that are different in your programme. If your company is reusing its BMS from a previous EV, set BMS Core to "Heavy reuse". If you are building a brand-new ADAS system, keep Camera Perception at "Fresh". Leave everything else at the preset defaults.

5
Understanding the 7 Software Categories

The 49 modules are grouped into 7 functional categories. Here is what each one means in plain English:

A · EV Powertrain & Battery (9 modules)
All the software that manages the battery pack, electric motors, and energy flow. Includes the Battery Management System (BMS) which monitors each cell's voltage and temperature, charging control, regenerative braking, and inverter control. This is usually the most safety-critical category because battery failures can cause fires.
B · ADAS L2/L2+ (8 modules)
Advanced Driver Assistance Systems. Software that uses cameras, radar, and ultrasonic sensors to help the driver — lane keeping, automatic emergency braking (AEB), adaptive cruise control, and highway assist. This is usually the most expensive category due to AI complexity and ASIL-D requirements.
C · Infotainment & UX (6 modules)
The touchscreen, navigation, voice assistant (like Hey BMW or Hey Mercedes), Bluetooth, and the visual interface the driver sees. Usually QM or ASIL-A — not safety critical, but very expensive because it needs to be polished and user-friendly.
D · Vehicle Domain Controllers (5 modules)
The "brains" that coordinate different systems. The Body Control Module (BCM) manages lights, windows, wipers. The Gateway ECU routes messages between different parts of the car. Modern "Zonal Architecture" replaces many small ECUs with a few powerful computers.
E · Middleware & Platform (6 modules)
The "operating system layer" underneath everything else. AUTOSAR is an industry-standard software framework that ensures all suppliers can connect their code together. Without AUTOSAR, every OEM would need to write bespoke interfaces for every ECU — massively expensive. This category is often heavily reused across programmes.
F · Cybersecurity — ISO 21434 (5 modules)
Software that protects the vehicle from hacking. Secure boot ensures only authorised software can run. Encryption protects data. The Intrusion Detection System (IDS) monitors for attacks in real time. This category has grown massively since connected cars became hackable via 4G/5G.
G · OTA & Cloud Backend (4 modules)
Over-the-Air (OTA) update software allows the manufacturer to push new features and bug fixes to the car remotely — just like your phone updates apps. The Cloud Backend manages this at scale across a million+ vehicles. Also includes the Data Pipeline that collects anonymised driving data to improve AI models.
6
Step 6 — Click Calculate and Read the Results

Once you have set your programme parameters and adjusted any modules, scroll down and click the Calculate Programme Cost button. Results appear immediately below in several sections:

📊 Summary Cards (top)
Four headline numbers: Total NRE (the full programme investment to develop the software), Per-Vehicle SW Cost (what this adds to the price of each car), Annual Maintenance (ongoing yearly cost after launch), and Team Size (estimated number of engineers needed).
🎲 Monte Carlo — P10 / P50 / P90
Software projects are notoriously hard to estimate. The Monte Carlo analysis runs 1,000 random simulations to give you a range instead of a single number:
  • P10 — Best case. Only 1-in-10 programmes come in this cheap. Use for optimistic board presentations.
  • P50 — Most likely. Half of programmes cost more, half cost less. Use this as your central estimate.
  • P90 — Worst case. 9-in-10 programmes will cost less than this. Use for risk/contingency planning.
📅 Programme Phases
Shows how the NRE budget is distributed across the development timeline. The 5 phases are: Feasibility (5%), Concept/Architecture (15%), Series Development (50%), Validation & V&V (20%), Ramp/SOP (10%). Use this to plan annual budget allocations.
🏷 Cost Composition
Breaks down the total NRE into 9 buckets: Development, Testing, Integration, Toolchain Licences, IP Licences, Cybersecurity, Calibration, Maintenance, and Cloud. Shows where the money actually goes — usually Development is 50–60% of total.
📉 Sensitivity Analysis
Shows which inputs have the biggest impact on cost. If a bar is very long, that variable (e.g. labour region or ASIL level) is a key cost driver. Useful for negotiation: "If we move development to Eastern Europe, cost drops by £X."
🏆 OEM Benchmarks
Compares your estimate to 7 real EV programmes (BMW iX, Tesla Model S, Porsche Taycan, Mercedes EQS, Range Rover, Audi Q8, Lucid Air). A green bar means your estimate is below market. A red bar means you are estimating more than those OEMs spent — worth investigating why.
🏭 OEM vs Tier-1 vs Startup Decomposition
Different types of companies have very different cost structures. This panel shows how your total would split if it were developed by a large OEM (most expensive, most rigorous), a Tier-1 supplier (mid-range), or a software startup (cheapest but least automotive-grade). Useful for make-vs-buy decisions.
7
Step 7 — Save Your Configuration

You can save your current set of inputs and come back to it later — useful for comparing "what if" scenarios side by side (e.g. "UK development" vs "India development").

  1. After setting your inputs (you do not need to calculate first), click the 💾 Save Config button at the top of the panel.
  2. Enter a name for this scenario (e.g. "Baseline UK", "Aggressive Offshore").
  3. The saved scenario appears in the Saved Configurations list above the presets.
  4. To restore it later, click Load next to its name — all inputs snap back to exactly what you saved.
  5. To delete it, click the ✕ button next to its name.

Note: Saved configurations are stored in your browser's local storage. They persist between sessions on the same device but will not transfer to another computer. You can save up to 10 configurations.

8
Step 8 — Export Your Results

After calculating, scroll to the bottom of the results panel to find the export buttons:

📥 Export Excel — Downloads a 6-sheet XLSX workbook:
  • Summary — headline numbers and programme parameters
  • Category Breakdown — cost by the 7 software categories
  • Module Detail — line-by-line cost for all 49 modules with ASIL, complexity, reuse settings
  • Sensitivity & Monte Carlo — P10/P50/P90 and sensitivity tornado
  • Benchmarks — your estimate vs 7 OEM comparators
  • Configuration — full record of every input you used
🤖 Get AI Analysis — Sends your results to an AI and returns a written narrative explaining the key cost drivers, risks, and recommendations in plain English. Useful for presenting to non-technical stakeholders.
📑 Board-level comparison reports (buttons in the Demo Programmes row)

Four ready-made, apple-to-apple reports (real drivetrains only) open in a new tab and print to PDF:

🟢 L460 Competitive Benchmark — one car vs its real rivals (BMW X7 / Audi Q8 / Mercedes GLS on MHEV; Porsche Cayenne on PHEV/BEV), with the cost-optimisation opportunity.
🔬 L460 Deep-Dive (all 49) — every module for one car: features, full cost stack, competitive delta and an insight each.
📊 All-Models Comparison — every module priced across all five models side by side, ranked leanest→dearest.
📈 Powertrain Cost Study — 5 cars × 4 drivetrains (ICE/MHEV/PHEV/BEV) in one matrix.
📋 Quick Reference — All Multipliers & Rates

ASIL Development Multipliers

QM1.00× — No safety req.
ASIL-A1.35× — Low hazard
ASIL-B1.80× — Medium hazard
ASIL-C2.30× — High hazard
ASIL-D3.20× — Max hazard

Complexity Multipliers

Low0.60×
Medium1.00× (baseline)
High1.70×
Very High2.80×

Reuse Factors

Fresh1.00× — all new
Light0.82×
Medium0.60×
Heavy0.35×
Platform0.14×

Regional Labour Rates (vs UK £28k/PM)

UK1.00× — £28k/PM
EU0.95× — £26.6k/PM
USA SV1.85× — £51.8k/PM
Japan0.90× — £25.2k/PM
E. Europe0.45× — £12.6k/PM
China0.35× — £9.8k/PM
Mexico0.28× — £7.8k/PM
India0.20× — £5.6k/PM
🏆 OEM Benchmark Reference

Your estimate is automatically compared against these 7 real EV programmes (based on analyst reports, teardown studies, and published financial disclosures):

BMW iX
Total NRE: £620M
Per vehicle: £4,800
Flagship EV with full in-house SW

Total NRE: £480M

Lucid Air
