Per vehicle: £7,800
Startup — ultra-low volume, high cost/car
❓ Frequently Asked Questions
Q: I changed a setting but the number did not change — what happened?
A: You need to click Calculate Programme Cost again after any change. The results do not update automatically.
Q: What is the difference between NRE and Per-Vehicle Cost?
A: NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) is the one-time total cost to develop the software — paid once. Per-Vehicle Cost is what that development investment adds to the price of each car when divided by total volume. A £500M NRE across 100,000 vehicles = £5,000 per vehicle.
Q: My estimate is much higher than the BMW benchmark. Is that wrong?
A: Not necessarily. BMW has decades of reusable code and global teams. If your programme is greenfield with a small team, higher costs are expected. Check the Sensitivity Analysis to see which inputs are driving the difference.
Q: Can I include only some modules and exclude others?
A: All 49 modules are included by default. To effectively "exclude" a module, set its Reuse to Platform reuse (0.14×) and Complexity to Low — this reduces its cost to near-zero, representing a module that is fully reused with no development effort.
Q: What does the orange ASIL warning badge mean?
A: It means you have selected a lower ASIL level than the engineering default for that module. For example, setting ASIL-A on an AEB braking system (which should be ASIL-D). The calculation still runs — it is a warning, not a block — but you should review whether that assumption is intentional.
Q: What currency is used?
A: All costs are in British Pounds (£ GBP) by default. Use the Currency dropdown in the top navigation bar to convert display values to EUR, USD, or other currencies.
🎬 Demo Script & Timeline (for a screen-recorded walkthrough)

Use the interactive player above to learn the feature. If you want to record a polished screen-capture video for the team, this is the ready-to-read narration script with on-screen actions and timings — a ~3-minute runtime.

TimeOn screen — what to doSay this (narration)
0:00–0:15Dashboard. Hover the top nav."This is CostVision. Today I'll price the entire software stack of a premium EV — from scratch — in about three minutes."
0:15–0:30Click ⊕ New Costing → Software → 💻 Auto SW Cost."New Costing, scroll to Software, and open Auto SW Cost. It opens in Guided mode — the six-step wizard, perfect if you're new."
0:30–0:55Step 1. Set Region = UK, Volume = 80k, Life = 10."Step one, context. Region sets our labour rate. Volume and programme life decide how the one-time engineering cost spreads across each car."
0:55–1:15Step 2. Leave all 7 domains ticked; point at ADAS & Powertrain."Step two, domains. A full programme includes all seven areas — powertrain, ADAS, infotainment, and so on. Untick any you don't ship."
1:15–1:40Step 3. Change ADAS reuse to Fresh, Powertrain to Medium."Step three — complexity, safety, reuse. Reuse is the biggest lever: building ADAS fresh is expensive; reusing a proven battery platform is cheap. ASIL is auto-set to safe ISO 26262 defaults."
1:40–2:05Step 4. Point at the per-domain cost table and the three KPI cards."Step four, and instantly we get effort and cost per domain — about £600 million total, right in the premium-EV band between Audi and BMW."
2:05–2:25Step 5. Nudge Overhead; show the sensitivity lines."Step five lets us fine-tune and see sensitivity — how much moving region or safety level swings the cost. Great for negotiation."
2:25–2:50Step 6. Show P10/P50/P90, benchmarks, click Generate AI."Step six is the report: a Monte Carlo range instead of a false single number, benchmarks against seven real programmes, and a one-click AI executive summary."
2:50–3:05Click Export Excel; open the Rate Library tab."Export to Excel or PDF for the review — and every rate is sourced in the Rate Library, so the number is defensible. That's a full should-cost in three minutes."

Recording tips: 1080p, capture just the browser window, 100% zoom. Record narration separately for clean audio. Pause ~1s after each click so viewers can follow. The 👑 Premium OEM preset (Advanced mode) is a good "what a real flagship costs" bonus clip.

📰
What is the News Feed?

The News section in the left sidebar aggregates live articles from 30 curated RSS sources covering automotive manufacturing, battery technology, commodity markets, EV technology, robotics, and supply chain. Up to 120 articles are fetched and displayed, refreshed automatically every 15 minutes.

  • Click 📰 News in the nav bar to open the full news view.
  • A live news ticker scrolls the latest headlines across the top of the screen.
  • The first article always displays as a featured hero card spanning the full grid width — larger image, expanded summary, and priority placement.
🔍
Search & Filter
  • Keyword Search — type in the search bar (top-right of the news view) to filter articles by title or summary in real time. Press Escape to clear.
  • Category Filters — click any category pill beneath the search bar to filter by topic. Each pill shows the live article count in brackets. Available categories: Battery, EV Tech, Automotive, Manufacturing, Materials, Cost & Commodity, Plastics, Robotics, Supply Chain, and more.
  • Combine search + category to narrow results further. The empty-state screen shows Clear search and ↻ Refresh feed buttons.
⏱
Timestamps & Reading Time

Every article card shows:

  • Relative timestamp — e.g. "just now", "14m ago", "3h ago", "Yesterday", "4d ago"
  • Reading time — estimated minutes to read, shown as a subtle badge (e.g. "2 min read")
  • Source name — the publication that published the article
  • Image thumbnail — pulled from the RSS feed's og:image or media enclosure; falls back to a colour gradient when unavailable
📡
News Sources (30 feeds)

Articles are sourced from industry-leading publications:

• Reuters Autos & Technology• Automotive News • Just Auto• Ward's Auto • SAE International• CleanTechnica • Charged EVs• Electrive • The Driven• Benchmark Mineral Intelligence • MetalMiner• Mining.com • Battery Industry• Supply Chain Dive • FreightWaves• Plastics News • Robotics247• Industry Today • Automotive News Mfg• + 11 more
↻
Refreshing the Feed
  • Auto-refresh — articles refresh automatically every 15 minutes in the background.
  • Manual refresh — click the ↻ Refresh button in the top-right corner of the news view to force an immediate reload.
  • The last updated timestamp (e.g. "Updated 3m ago") appears next to the article count.
  • Articles are filtered to automotive relevance using a 70+ keyword classifier — off-topic articles from general news RSS feeds are automatically excluded.
📈
Live Commodity Price Dashboard

The Live Prices panel provides real-time commodity intelligence for 52 automotive-relevant materials. Access it by clicking 📈 Live Prices in the navigation bar. The panel covers metals (steel, aluminium, copper, nickel, cobalt, lithium, rare earths), plastics (PP, ABS, PA66, PC, HDPE), and EV-specific materials (NMC, LFP, silicon carbide, electrical steel).

⚡
Live Ticker

A scrolling commodity ticker runs across the top of the screen (similar to a financial data terminal), showing current prices and 24h change for key materials. Colour coding: green = rising, red = falling, grey = unchanged. The ticker updates every 5 seconds with simulated live price movement.

📊
KPI Cards

Five summary KPI cards appear at the top of the dashboard:

  • Spot Price — current market price per unit (£/kg, £/t, £/troy oz)
  • 30-Day Change — price movement vs. 30 days ago (% and absolute)
  • 52-Week Range — min/max price over the past year
  • Should-Cost Impact — how much a 10% price move affects a typical part cost (£/part)
  • Availability Index — supply tightness score (0–100) based on inventory levels and demand
🌍
Region Risk Index

The Region Risk Bar displays a geopolitical supply risk score for each commodity by sourcing region. A score above 70 indicates elevated supply chain risk (e.g., lithium from a single country, cobalt from DRC). Use this to flag materials with dual-sourcing requirements in your cost model.

📉
Sparklines & Forecasting
  • Sparklines — mini price history charts showing 7, 30, or 90 days of price movement for each commodity.
  • 30-Day Forecast — AI-powered trend projection using Ornstein-Uhlenbeck mean-reversion modelling. Shown as a dashed line on the sparkline chart.
  • 90-Day Forecast — longer range projection; wider confidence bands. Use for annual budget planning and should-cost stress-testing.
  • Forecasts are statistical models, not guaranteed prices. Always validate against live broker quotes for high-value sourcing decisions.
🔔
Price Alerts

Set threshold alerts on any commodity:

  1. Click the 🔔 Alert button on a commodity card.
  2. Enter a price threshold (e.g., "Alert me if LME Aluminium exceeds £2,400/t").
  3. Choose direction: above or below threshold.
  4. When the live price crosses the threshold, a banner notification appears at the top of the screen.
  5. Alerts persist across sessions via browser localStorage.
💰
Should-Cost Integration

Every commodity in the dashboard has a Should-Cost Impact Coefficient — the multiplier that links commodity price to part cost. For example, if Hot-Rolled Steel rises 15%, the steel content of a stamped bracket increases proportionally. Use these coefficients to:

  • Quantify PPV exposure on existing supplier contracts
  • Run "what-if" sensitivity analyses (e.g., "what if nickel rises 20%?")
  • Identify which materials have the highest cost-risk for your portfolio

The impact coefficient is automatically populated into the cost model when you select a commodity material — the calculator uses live prices as the material rate baseline.

How do I change the manufacturing region?
Use the region dropdown in the top bar. Selecting a different country automatically adjusts machine rates, labour rates, energy costs, and material transport costs for that region. All rates are from the Rate Library which you can inspect and edit via Edit Rates.
Can I edit the rate library?
Yes. Click Edit Rates in the action bar. You can modify machine hourly rates, labour rates, and material prices. Changes apply immediately to the next Calculate run. Use Reset to Defaults to revert to the original published rates.
What is the 8-bucket cost model?
CostVision computes: Raw Material + Process + Labour + Tooling + Packaging + Logistics + Overhead + Margin. This mirrors the aPriori / Siemens Teamcenter Sourcing should-cost methodology used by Tier 1 automotive and aerospace OEMs.
What is Wright's Law / Learning Curve?
Wright's Law states that for every doubling of cumulative production volume, unit cost decreases by a fixed percentage (typically 10–20% for manufacturing). Enable the Apply learning curve checkbox and set the curve % (default 85% = 15% reduction per doubling). Set your annual volume and reference volume for the calculation.
How do I compare two designs?
Save each design as a scenario using Save Scenario. Then click the Scenarios tab in the results panel, select two scenarios, and click Compare. A delta breakdown table shows cost differences by bucket, with colour-coded savings/increases.
Are my scenarios saved if I close the browser?
Yes — CostVision uses IndexedDB for persistent browser storage. Scenarios survive page reloads and browser restarts. Use Export Scenarios to create a portable JSON backup file. If IndexedDB storage is full or unavailable, a warning toast will appear.
Why are there no articles in the News section?
This usually means the RSS feeds failed to load. Check your internet connection and try clicking ↻ Refresh. If search or a category filter is active, clear it using the Clear search button — your filter may be excluding all current articles. If the problem persists, the RSS sources may be temporarily unavailable; the feed will retry automatically within 15 minutes.
How often does the news feed update?
The server fetches from all 30 RSS sources and caches the result for 15 minutes. Each time you open the News view or click ↻ Refresh, the client checks for new server data. Articles are de-duplicated by URL and ranked by publication date (newest first).
What do the News category filters mean?
Articles are automatically classified into categories using a keyword classifier with 70+ terms:
  • Battery — lithium, cobalt, NMC, LFP, cell chemistries
  • EV Tech — electric vehicles, charging, inverters, SiC
  • Automotive — OEMs, platforms, model launches, recalls
  • Manufacturing — production, gigacast, tooling, supply chain
  • Materials — metals, rare earths, critical minerals
  • Cost & Commodity — LME prices, PPV, sourcing
  • Plastics — polymers, resins, compound prices
  • Robotics — automation, cobot, welding cells
How accurate are the Live Commodity Prices?
The commodity prices are derived from publicly available market data and simulated using Ornstein-Uhlenbeck mean-reversion models calibrated against real LME, Fastmarkets, and Bloomberg spot prices. They are intended for should-cost benchmarking and trend analysis, not for financial trading or contract pricing. For binding sourcing decisions, always verify against your broker's live quote.
Can I export the full report as a single PDF?
Yes. After running a calculation, scroll to the bottom of the Breakdown tab in the results panel and click Export Full Report PDF. This generates a comprehensive multi-section PDF covering all tabs: cost breakdown, sensitivity analysis, AI insights, material data, and scenario comparison. The report includes your part photo if one was uploaded.
OEE
Overall Equipment Effectiveness — uptime × performance × quality. Typical range 60–95%.
Cycle Time
Time per machine cycle in hours. Includes machining + handling. Parts per cycle divides this.
Manning
Number of operators per machine. Affects labour cost per part.
MOQ
Minimum Order Quantity — minimum batch a supplier will accept per purchase order.
NRE
Non-Recurring Engineering cost — one-time tooling, setup, or design cost.
PPV
Purchase Price Variance — % difference between quoted supplier price and the should-cost model.
Amortisation
Spreading tooling NRE cost over the expected production volume to compute per-part tooling cost.
Casting Yield
Ratio of finished part weight to total pour weight (runner + gate loss). Typically 0.6–0.95.
Labour Efficiency
Effective labour utilisation (0–1). Accounts for non-value-added time. Typical: 0.80–0.95.
DfM Score
Design for Manufacturability score (0–100) from AI CAD analysis. Higher = easier to manufacture.
Tornado Chart
Sensitivity analysis chart showing which input variables have the largest impact on total cost.
Wax Recovery
In investment casting, fraction of wax recovered via autoclave dewaxing. Default 80%.
!
"Scenario could not be saved" warning

Your browser's IndexedDB storage may be full or blocked. Go to browser settings → Site Data → clear storage for this site, then reload. Alternatively, export your scenarios to JSON before clearing.

!
AI Agent returns invalid material/machine IDs

The AI may suggest IDs not in the current rate library. Invalid IDs are automatically nulled and a warning toast is shown. Manually select the correct material or machine from the dropdown after the AI populates other fields.

!
PDF export is missing the Insights section

The §7 AI Insights section only appears if the AI Insights tab has generated recommendations (click Calculate, then view the Insights tab to trigger generation). Run Calculate before exporting the PDF.

!
Rate library shows "stale" warning

The rate library data is over 90 days old. Click Edit Rates to review and update material prices, machine rates, and labour rates to current market values.

!
Results show unexpectedly high material cost

Check the Material Utilisation field — a value less than 1 means significant scrap allowance. For casting, check Casting Yield and Reject Rate. Verify the Consumables cost per part is not accidentally large.

!
News feed shows "No articles found" after loading

This can happen for two reasons: (1) a search term or category filter with no matching articles — click Clear search and select All in the category bar; (2) all 30 RSS sources returned errors during the last fetch cycle. Click ↻ Refresh to trigger a new server-side fetch. If the problem persists beyond 2–3 minutes, the news server may be restarting — wait and retry. The news ticker will stop scrolling when no articles are available.

!
Live Commodity Prices appear stale or frozen

The commodity dashboard updates prices every 5 seconds via the browser's stochastic price engine. If values appear frozen: (1) check that the Live Prices panel is open and in the foreground (prices pause when the tab is hidden to save CPU); (2) reload the page — the price engine auto-restarts on load. The "last refreshed" timestamp in the panel header shows when data was last fetched from the server.

!
Price alert notification did not appear

Price alerts fire as in-app banner notifications only while the CostVision tab is open and active. They are not browser push notifications. If the commodity price crossed the threshold while the tab was in the background or closed, the alert will not trigger retroactively. Re-open the Live Prices dashboard and re-check the alert threshold — if the current price already exceeds it, adjust the threshold or delete and recreate the alert.

!
News ticker is not visible on the Home screen

The news ticker only appears once at least one article has been loaded. It is hidden by default if the news feed has not yet been fetched. Navigate to 📰 News once to trigger the initial RSS fetch — after that, the ticker will appear on the home screen and refresh automatically. If the ticker is still missing after opening News, check for console errors and try a hard reload (Ctrl+Shift+R).

V4.2
CostVision V4.2 — Auto SW Cost: Demos, Premium Modules & Comparison Reports
July 2026 · latest update to the Automotive Software Should-Cost model
🚗 What changed in Auto SW Cost LATEST
  • 49 modules — the catalogue grew from 43 to 49 with 6 premium-trim options (active body control, premium audio & ANC, automated parking, climate control, digital key UWB, AR-HUD). They default to off so the validated 43-module baseline is unchanged.
  • Demo Programmes — one-click, fully-configured real vehicles: Range Rover L460, BMW X7, Audi Q8, Mercedes GLS and Porsche Cayenne, each across its real drivetrains (ICE / MHEV / PHEV / BEV). Every demo auto-computes and offers a View full report.
  • Four comparison reports — Competitive Benchmark, module-by-module Deep-Dive (all 49), All-Models Comparison, and a Powertrain Cost Study (5 cars × 4 drivetrains) — apple-to-apple on real drivetrains only, exportable to PDF.
  • Accuracy stated — validated to a ±25–35% envelope (back-test MAPE 24.9%, 5 of 7 published programmes within ±35%). Every figure is live engine output.
  • Engine refinements — ASIL-scaled testing effort, complexity broadened beyond the algorithm bucket, safety-effort reuse floor, and optional levers (schedule compression, NPV, cost-recovery window, ML-data cost, programme homologation).
V4.1
CostVision V4.1 — Automotive Software Should-Cost + Animation Layer
June 2026 · 4 major features · 19+ calculators · 49-module EV software cost model
New in V4.1
🚗 Automotive Software Should-Cost NEW

A complete parameter-driven software cost model for premium luxury EV programmes (2024–2026). 49 modules across 7 categories: EV Powertrain & Battery, ADAS L2+, Infotainment/UX, Domain Controllers, Middleware/Platform, Cybersecurity (ISO 21434), and OTA/Cloud Backend.

  • ASIL multipliers (QM → D) for both development and testing phases
  • 9 regional labour rates from India (0.20×) to USA Silicon Valley (1.85×)
  • Complexity & Reuse factors — Low to Very High, Fresh to Platform
  • 9 cost dimensions: Dev, Test, Integration, Toolchain, Cybersec, Maintenance, Cloud, IP Licensing, per-vehicle amortisation
  • Sensitivity analysis across 6 parameters (ASIL, complexity, reuse, region, programme life, volume)
  • Benchmark comparison against BMW iX, Porsche Taycan, Mercedes EQS, Range Rover L460, Tesla HW4, Audi Q8 e-tron, Lucid Air
  • 4 quick-set presets: Aggressive, Industry Baseline, Premium OEM, Offshored
  • PDF export with professional navy/blue report
✨ Framer Motion Animation Layer NEW

A complete spring-physics animation layer built with the motion package (Framer Motion's vanilla DOM library). Adds dynamic, hardware-accelerated motion to every interaction without requiring React.

  • Hover lift & scale — dashboard cards, KPI tiles, commodity tiles, result cards all spring-lift on hover with drop shadow
  • Press haptic — buttons compress slightly on press using snappy spring physics
  • InView reveal — sections and rows fade+slide in as they enter the viewport
  • Staggered row reveals — table rows enter in cascade sequence on result render
  • Hero parallax — login page hero image scrolls at 55% speed for depth
  • Cursor glow — a soft radial gradient follows the mouse on the hero section
  • Panel open/close spring — costing panels animate open and close with bounce easing
🎬 GSAP Animation Layer NEW

Professional animation orchestration using GSAP (GreenSock Animation Platform) and ScrollTrigger for view lifecycle transitions and UI micro-interactions.

  • View transitions — dashboard, costing, and results views animate in/out on navigation
  • Card tilt — dashboard cards follow the mouse pointer with a subtle 3D perspective tilt
  • Button ripple — primary action buttons emit a CSS ripple on click
  • ScrollTrigger counters — KPI numbers count up from zero when they scroll into view
  • Toast spring — notification toasts slide in from top with spring easing and auto-dismiss
  • Chat spring — AI chat messages drop in with a bouncy spring for each new message
  • Dashboard stagger — stat cards and chart sections stagger into view on dashboard render
📄 Professional PDF Report Redesign IMPROVED

Every PDF report has been completely redesigned with a professional deep-navy + orange brand theme, fixing all text overlap and column overflow issues from the previous version.

  • Cover page — deep navy banner (68mm) with orange accent stripe, CV logo box, and part summary card with confidence band
  • Column widths fixed — all 10 table sections recalculated to sum exactly to 182mm (no more text spilling outside cells)
  • §4 Operations split — the 13-column operations table split into two focused tables (Machine Ops 6 cols, Labour Detail 8 cols) to prevent overflow
  • CAD Analysis PDF redesigned — teal/navy professional theme with fixed column widths
  • Confidence band computed — overall report confidence (High/Medium/Low) derived from traceability record mix rather than a missing field
  • Cleaner section headers, consistent font sizing, alternating row shading throughout
Earlier Releases
V4.0 May 2026 Major
  • New Costing & Workflow Panel — gradient ⊕ button opens full-width commodity picker with 19+ processes; costing slides in as a 52%-width right panel
  • Live Commodity Dashboard — real-time prices for 52 automotive commodities with sparklines, AI forecasts, and price alert thresholds
  • Luxury SUV Demo Gallery — 50+ pre-configured automotive examples across all commodity types, one-click load with full AI insights
  • AI Cost Assistant — floating chat with real-time manufacturing cost engineering chatbot, commodity-aware and context-sensitive
V3.5 March 2026 Feature
  • News & Market Intelligence — 120 articles from 30 curated RSS feeds; live news ticker, keyword search, category filter pills, 15-min auto-refresh
  • PCB Image-to-BOM — upload a PCB photo and AI extracts the full Bill of Materials, populates PCB Fab and PCBA forms directly
  • Country for Costing — region selector inside every form updates labour rates, machine rates, material prices, and overhead % instantly
  • Quick Compare — tick 2 rows in Recent Costings to compare bucket-level cost differences with colour-coded Δ values
V3.0 January 2026 Major
  • CAD Analysis AI — upload STEP/STL/DXF/photos and get AI-powered should-cost estimates with manufacturability feedback
  • Scenario Manager — save, compare, and version-control costing scenarios with full Δ analysis across all 8 cost buckets
  • Excel Export — 6-sheet workbooks with full rate buildup, sensitivity tables, and traceability records
⌨ Keyboard Shortcuts Reference

These shortcuts work anywhere on the page — except when the cursor is inside a text field, select, or textarea.

Navigation
⊕ New Costing N
🏠 Dashboard D
Result Tab 1 — Breakdown 1
Result Tab 2 — Operations 2
Result Tab 3 — Sensitivity 3
Result Tab 4 — AI Insights 4
Result Tab 5 — Traceability 5
Close panel / modal Esc
Actions
Open Help Centre ?
Open Help (alternate) Shift+H
Export PDF Report Ctrl+E
Export PDF (Mac) ⌘+E
💡 Tips
  • Shortcuts are automatically suppressed while typing — no need to worry about conflicts with text fields.
  • Press ? from anywhere on the main dashboard to jump back to this Help Centre.
  • Tabs 1–5 switch result view tabs after a calculation — quickly flip between cost breakdown, operations detail, sensitivity analysis, AI insights, and traceability.
  • Use Ctrl+E / ⌘+E to export a PDF report without using the mouse.
Overview — Injection Moulding

Injection moulding is a high-volume thermoplastic process. A heated screw injects molten polymer into a steel or aluminium tool. Cycle times are typically 15–90 seconds. Cost is dominated by tooling amortisation at low-to-medium volumes and material at high volumes.

🆕 Latest features (2026): parametric mould-cost estimator (by cavities / steel grade / complexity); IMM machine ladder 50–1200T with clamp-tonnage validation warnings; expanded resin library (PC/ABS, PMMA, HIPS, LCP + glass-fill ladder) with auto cool-factor from resin thermal diffusivity; injection-moulding DFM advisor; technician & supervisor labour categories; family-aware regional resin pricing.
Inputs Required
  • Material grade (PP, ABS, PC/ABS, PA66-GF30, etc.) and part weight (kg)
  • Projected area (cm²) and tool type (single/multi-cavity, hot or cold runner)
  • Machine tonnage (determined by projected area × cavity pressure)
  • Cycle time (s), OEE (%), cavities per shot
  • Tooling cost (£) and amortisation volume
  • Surface finish and tolerance class (affects multiplier)
  • Annual volume (pcs) and manufacturing region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select Injection tab or use ⊕ New Costing → Plastics tile.
  2. Choose the material grade and enter part weight. The tool auto-prices material from the rate library.
  3. Enter projected area and cavity count. Machine tonnage is auto-recommended.
  4. Enter cycle time (shoot time + cooling + eject). A typical 0.3 kg part runs 35–55 s.
  5. Enter OEE (typically 75–90%), Manning (usually 0.25–0.5 per machine), and parts per cycle.
  6. Enter tooling cost and amortisation volume. For 50k ppa, a single-cavity ABS tool is typically £18–45k.
  7. Set annual volume, overhead %, margin %, and region, then click Calculate.
Tips
  • Add a 1.08–1.15× surface finish multiplier for textured or Class A surfaces.
  • Multi-cavity tools reduce per-part cost but increase tooling NRE — break-even is usually above 30k ppa.
  • Hot runners reduce cycle time and scrap but add £8–25k to tooling cost.
Common Mistakes
  • Forgetting runner/gate scrap — add 5–15% material uplift for cold runner tools.
  • Under-estimating cycle time for thick-wall or large parts (rule: 1 s per mm wall thickness minimum).
  • Using a 1-cavity assumption when the supplier will almost certainly use 2-cavity for volumes above 20k.
Overview — Sheet Metal Fabrication

Sheet metal fabrication covers laser cutting, turret punching, press brake bending, and optional welding. It is a low-tooling, high-flexibility route suited to low-to-medium volumes (1–50k). Cost is labour-intensive and driven by machine time, bends, and weld length.

🆕 Latest features (2026): parametric stamping die-cost estimator + die-life predictor; fine-blanking route; stamping DFM advisor; press ladder to 1250T (+ fine-blank press); expanded grades (electrical, spring, deep-draw steels) and hot-stamping / press-hardening; multiple secondary operations; e-motor lamination costing (all electrical-steel grades — NO / EV-thin / GO / CoFe / NiFe / amorphous — join methods, anneal & coating, lamination DFM); and advanced 2026 EV materials (giga-cast HTF, high-speed rotor steel, hairpin copper, busbar — incl. BYD / Xiaomi flagship grades).
Inputs Required
  • Material grade (DC01, S355, 304 SS, 5052 Al) and sheet thickness (mm)
  • Blank dimensions (mm × mm) and part weight (kg)
  • Blanking method: Laser (Fiber / CO₂), Turret punch, or Shear
  • Number of bends and bend length (mm) for press brake
  • Welding: type (MIG, TIG, spot), length (mm), and number of joints
  • Surface treatment (powder coat, galvanise, primer)
  • Annual volume and manufacturing region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select SM Fab ✓ tab or use ⊕ New Costing → Sheet Metal tile.
  2. Choose material grade and enter sheet thickness. The process advisor recommends a blanking method.
  3. Enter blank size and part weight. Material cost is auto-calculated including nesting utilisation.
  4. Select laser machine (fiber for stainless / non-ferrous; CO₂ for mild steel below 8 mm).
  5. Enter cut perimeter (mm) — the tool calculates cut time from machine speed.
  6. Add press brake operations: number of bends, bend length, setup time.
  7. Add weld operations if required (MIG seam / spot weld / TIG).
  8. Set volume, overhead, margin, and click Calculate.
Tips
  • Fiber laser is 3× faster than CO₂ for stainless and aluminium — always choose fiber for these materials.
  • Spot welding is 5–10× cheaper than MIG for overlapping flanges — recommend DFM change if possible.
  • Powder coating adds £1.50–4.00/part for small brackets; include it in universal costs.
Common Mistakes
  • Forgetting pierce time for hole features — each pierce adds 1–3 s on a laser.
  • Under-estimating sheet nesting efficiency (typically 65–80%); use 0.72 as default material utilisation.
  • Ignoring setup time for small batches — a press brake setup is 20–45 min regardless of volume.
Overview — Castings (HPDC / Sand)

Casting fills a die or mould with molten metal. HPDC (High Pressure Die Casting) uses permanent steel dies for aluminium/zinc at high volumes. Sand casting uses expendable moulds for iron/steel/aluminium at lower volumes or large parts. Cost is driven by die cost, cycle time, and yield.

Inputs Required
  • Alloy (ADC12, A380, GJL250, EN-GJS-400) and process (HPDC, sand, gravity, investment)
  • Part weight (kg) and casting yield (typically 0.70–0.95 for HPDC)
  • Machine tonnage (HPDC: use projected area × 600 bar / 1000 as starting point)
  • Cycle time (s), number of cavities, OEE (%)
  • Die/pattern cost (£) and amortisation volume
  • Reject rate (%), post-cast operations (shot blast, impregnation, heat treat)
  • Annual volume and region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select Casting tab or ⊕ New Costing → Castings tile.
  2. Choose alloy and casting process. The model sets appropriate material price and yield defaults.
  3. Enter part weight and casting yield. Poured weight = part weight ÷ yield.
  4. Select machine tonnage. For HPDC: typical 800T die cast produces 2–6 kg parts.
  5. Enter cycle time (HPDC typical: 25–60 s; sand cast: 5–30 min per pour).
  6. Enter die cost. HPDC dies: £80–350k; sand cast patterns: £5–40k.
  7. Enter reject rate (HPDC: 2–5%; sand: 5–12%) and post-cast treatment costs.
  8. Click Calculate to get the full as-cast cost breakdown.
Tips
  • For cast + machined parts, use the Cast+Machine ✓ tab which combines both in one model.
  • HPDC at 2-cavity doubles output without proportionally doubling die cost — check cavity economics above 40k ppa.
  • Investment casting is more expensive per part but achieves ±0.1 mm tolerances without machining.
Common Mistakes
  • Confusing part weight with poured weight — always model poured weight for material cost.
  • Ignoring heat treatment cost for structural aluminium castings (T5/T6 adds £0.40–1.20/kg).
  • Underestimating HPDC die life — budget for 1 die replacement per 100k shots for aluminium.
Overview — CNC Machining

CNC machining removes material from bar, billet, or casting stock using turning centres, VMCs (3/4/5-axis), and grinders. Cost is dominated by machine hourly rate × cycle time. Material waste (buy-to-fly ratio) can be significant for aerospace or complex geometries.

Inputs Required
  • Material (Al6061-T6, 304 SS, Ti-6Al-4V) and billet/bar weight (kg)
  • Finished part weight (kg) — determines buy-to-fly ratio
  • Operations list: machine type, cycle time (h), OEE, Manning
  • Tooling cost per operation (cutting tools, fixtures)
  • Annual volume and region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select Machining tab or ⊕ New Costing → Machining tile.
  2. Choose material grade. Enter billet weight (raw input) and finished part weight.
  3. Add Operation 1: select machine (VMC 3-axis, Turn/Mill, 5-axis DMG), enter cycle time and OEE.
  4. Add further operations as required (e.g., Op 2: drill and tap on HMC).
  5. Enter per-operation tooling consumable cost (typical: £0.50–3.00 per part for Al, £2–8 for steel).
  6. Set Manning ratio (usually 0.25–1.0 depending on automation level).
  7. Set volume, overhead, margin, region, and click Calculate.
Tips
  • UK 3-axis VMC rates: £55–85/hr; 5-axis: £90–140/hr. Use Edit Rates to customise.
  • For Al parts, OEE of 82–88% is typical. For titanium, expect 65–75% due to tool change frequency.
  • Buy-to-fly above 3:1 signals a DFM opportunity — consider near-net casting or forging.
Common Mistakes
  • Forgetting setup amortisation — a complex 5-axis fixture setup can add £0.50–2.00/part at 10k volume.
  • Using finished weight instead of billet weight for material cost calculation.
  • Assuming 100% OEE — always apply real-world OEE (75–90%) to avoid under-costing.
Overview — Forgings

Forging plastically deforms metal (hot or warm) into a die to produce high-strength near-net-shape parts. Closed-die forging produces tighter tolerances than open-die. Cost is driven by material, press tonnage, die cost, and heat treatment. Forgings have superior grain structure vs. castings.

🆕 Latest features (2026): billet-heating energy now costed; forging-force estimate with press-tonnage validation; parametric die-cost estimator + die-life predictor; forge machine / tonnage ladder; family-aware metal pricing (exchange-traded vs mill); NDT / coining / furnace operations; superalloy / Ti / Al / Mg billets; multi-step (preform) forging; and a forging DFMA advisor.
Inputs Required
  • Material (Al6082, 4340 Steel, Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718)
  • Billet weight (kg) and finished forging weight (kg) — flash loss typically 10–25%
  • Process (open-die, closed-die, near-net-shape) and press tonnage
  • Number of blows/strikes, die cost (£), amortisation volume
  • Heat treatment requirement (T4, T6, normalise, quench+temper)
  • Annual volume and region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select Forging tab or ⊕ New Costing → Forgings tile.
  2. Choose material and enter billet weight. Add flash allowance (15% typical for closed-die Al).
  3. Select press tonnage. Rule of thumb: projected area (cm²) × 3 = tonnes required for Al; ×5 for steel.
  4. Enter number of strikes (typically 3–6 for complex closed-die). Each strike has a machine cycle time.
  5. Enter die cost: Al forgings typically £40–120k; steel £70–180k. Die life: 20–80k shots.
  6. Add heat treatment if required (T6 for aluminium adds £0.60–1.50/kg).
  7. Calculate and review tooling amortisation — major cost driver at low volumes.
Tips
  • Forging vs. casting: forgings are 20–40% stronger but 30–80% more expensive per part — justify with fatigue or safety requirements.
  • Near-net-shape forgings can eliminate machining steps, reducing total cost by 15–25%.
  • China forging: 35–55% lower piece price but factor in 8–12 week lead time and quality risk.
Common Mistakes
  • Forgetting flash trimming cost — typically 10–20% of forging cycle cost for closed-die parts.
  • Under-sizing the press — always add 20% safety margin to minimum required tonnage.
  • Ignoring die maintenance cost at high volumes — budget 15–20% of die cost for lifetime maintenance.
Overview — PCB Fabrication

PCB fabrication converts a bare laminate into a circuit board via lamination, drilling, plating, etching, solder mask, and surface finish. Cost is driven by layer count, board area, HDI complexity, surface finish, and quality grade. Use the 🔬 Image-to-BOM feature to auto-extract specs from a photo.

Inputs Required
  • Board dimensions (mm × mm) and layer count (2, 4, 6, 8+)
  • Technology type (FR4 standard, High-Tg FR4, HDI, Rigid-Flex, RF/Rogers)
  • Surface finish (HASL, HASL-LF, ENIG, OSP, ENEPIG)
  • Via counts: through, blind, buried, micro-vias
  • HDI structure (none, 1+N+1, 2+N+2, any-layer)
  • Quality grade (consumer, industrial, automotive Gr.2, Gr.1, aerospace)
  • Annual volume (panels) and region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select PCB Fab 🔬 tab or ⊕ New Costing → PCB tile.
  2. Optional: upload a PCB photo via the 🔬 Image-to-BOM zone — the AI auto-fills all fields.
  3. Enter board dimensions and layer count. Each pair of inner layers adds 35–55% to fab cost.
  4. Select technology type. HDI costs 2.5–4× standard FR4 due to laser drilling of microvias.
  5. Select surface finish. ENIG is standard for automotive; HASL-LF for consumer cost reduction.
  6. Enter via counts. Blind/buried vias add 20–60% cost uplift vs. through-vias only.
  7. Select quality grade. Automotive Gr.1/2 adds 25–80% to base fab cost for testing and traceability.
  8. Click Calculate for per-panel and per-board cost with min/mid/max range.
Tips
  • PCB cost scales with area² — halving the board size reduces fab cost by 60–70%.
  • ENIG is worth the premium over HASL for fine-pitch BGAs and SMD pads below 0.5 mm pitch.
  • Panel utilisation (default 75%) — tighter layouts increase this and reduce per-board cost.
Common Mistakes
  • Confusing layer count with copper layer count — a 4-layer board has 2 inner + 2 outer layers.
  • Selecting ENEPIG when ENIG is sufficient — ENEPIG costs 40–60% more and is only needed for wire bonding.
  • Forgetting flying probe or ICT test cost — add £0.15–0.60 per board for automotive quality grades.
Overview — PCBA Assembly

PCBA assembly involves SMT placement (solder paste → pick-and-place → reflow), through-hole soldering (wave or selective), and optional processes (BGA underfill, conformal coat, ICT, X-ray). Cost is driven by component count, placement complexity, BGA presence, and quality grade.

🆕 Latest features (2026): PCB Image-to-BOM accuracy layer — optional BOM / pick-and-place file import (Altium / KiCad CPL), a deterministic post-processor (component-type & MPN normalisation, passive detection, per-line confidence + cost range), and self-consistency merge across multiple AI extractions.
Inputs Required
  • BOM: component type, quantity, unit price, automotive grade flag
  • SMT placements (total qty of all SMT components)
  • Through-hole joints (qty × pin count for THT components)
  • BGA count (drives X-ray requirement)
  • Assembly complexity (low/medium/high/very high)
  • Reflow sides (1 or 2), conformal coat area (cm²)
  • Quality grade and ICT test time (s)
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select PCBA 🔬 tab or ⊕ New Costing → PCBA tile.
  2. Optional: upload a PCB photo to auto-extract BOM via AI Image-to-BOM.
  3. Review or enter the BOM. Mark any automotive-grade components (3–5× price premium).
  4. Enter assembly parameters: SMT placements, TH joints, BGA count.
  5. Select complexity (high = fine-pitch QFN/BGA, double-sided, tight spacing).
  6. Add ICT test time (s) — automotive Gr.2 minimum is 90 s; Gr.1 is 180+ s.
  7. Enter conformal coat area if applicable (automotive standard IPC-CC-830).
  8. Click Calculate for per-board PCBA cost split by BOM, SMT, and test.
Tips
  • CPH (components per hour) for a modern high-speed SMT line: 40,000–80,000 CPH.
  • BGA rework is £8–25 each — minimise BGA count or specify sockets for prototypes.
  • AEC-Q100/Q200 automotive parts cost 3–8× the consumer equivalent — always flag these in the BOM.
Common Mistakes
  • Forgetting NRE (stencil, programming, first article inspection) — typically £350–800 one-time.
  • Using consumer IC pricing for automotive designs — always verify AEC-Q grade pricing.
  • Under-stating placement count — count every pad on every SMT component including decoupling caps.
Overview — Wiring Harness

A wiring harness bundles multiple wire circuits into a routed assembly with connectors, terminals, splices, conduit, and tape. Cost is labour-intensive (60–75% of total cost for simple harnesses). Assembly is largely manual, making labour rate and assembly time the primary cost drivers.

Inputs Required
  • Wire circuits: gauge (mm²), length per circuit (m), insulation type
  • Connector types and pin counts (Delphi MX150, TE AMP, Molex, etc.)
  • Splices, seals, and terminal count
  • Conduit/corrugated tube length (m) and tape area (m²)
  • Assembly time estimate (hours per harness)
  • Test type (continuity, HiPot, functional)
  • Annual volume and manufacturing region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select Harness ✓ tab or ⊕ New Costing → Wiring Harness tile.
  2. Add wire circuits: for each circuit, enter gauge, length, and insulation type.
  3. Add connectors: housing unit price, terminal count, and seal cost.
  4. Enter conduit length and tape area. Tape is typically 120% of circuit length.
  5. Enter assembly time (h). A 10-circuit door harness typically takes 0.25–0.45 h to assemble.
  6. Select test type. HiPot test adds £0.05–0.15 per harness; functional adds £0.20–0.60.
  7. Set region — harness assembly in Romania/Morocco is 40–60% lower labour cost than UK.
  8. Click Calculate for material, assembly, and test cost breakdown.
Tips
  • For automotive harnesses, use 0.35–0.5 mm² for signal circuits and 1.5–4.0 mm² for power circuits.
  • Splice usage: each splice saves a connector pin but adds crimping time — net saving only above 5k volume.
  • Romania and Morocco rates are £3.50–5.50/hr — benchmark for all volume automotive harness work.
Common Mistakes
  • Forgetting terminal crimp time — each crimp adds 4–8 s assembly time.
  • Under-estimating tape usage — use 1.2× circuit length for full wrap, 0.6× for partial wrap.
  • Ignoring drawing/BOM preparation NRE — a complex harness drawing takes 4–16 engineering hours.
Overview — Cast + Machine

Cast+Machine combines HPDC (or sand/gravity) casting with post-cast CNC machining in a single integrated model. This is the correct approach for any cast part that requires subsequent machining for critical dimensions, threads, or sealing faces. Do not model these separately.

Inputs Required
  • Casting: alloy, process, poured weight, die cost, machine tonnage, cycle time
  • Post-cast: heat treatment (T5, T6, HT, none), shot blast, impregnation
  • Machining operations: machine type, cycle time, OEE, tooling cost per op
  • Annual volume and region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select Cast+Machine ✓ tab or ⊕ New Costing → Cast + Machine tile.
  2. Complete the Casting section: alloy, process, poured/part weight, die cost, cycle time.
  3. Add post-cast operations: heat treatment (T6 for structural Al: +£0.80–1.50/kg), shot blast (£0.10–0.30/part), impregnation (£0.25–0.60/part).
  4. Add machining operations for critical features: bore diameter, face mill, drill and tap.
  5. For each machining op, select machine (3-axis HAAS, 5-axis DMG) and enter cycle time.
  6. Review the combined cost breakdown — casting typically represents 40–65% of total cost.
Tips
  • Move machining tolerances into the casting wherever possible — every machined feature adds £0.30–1.50 per part.
  • For sealing faces, minimum machining (0.5 mm stock removal) is faster and cheaper than full face milling.
  • Specify T5 instead of T6 where mechanical properties allow — saves 3–4 h heat treatment cycle time.
Common Mistakes
  • Costing the casting and machining separately — use Cast+Machine tab for accurate combined tooling amortisation.
  • Forgetting impregnation for HPDC housings — porosity in HPDC is common; impregnation is mandatory for fluid-tight parts.
  • Under-estimating fixturing time — a complex 5-axis fixture changeover can add 12–25 min per batch.
Overview — Assembly BOM Rollup

The Assembly BOM model computes total assembly cost from a component list plus assembly operations. It supports multi-level BOM rollup, sub-assembly costing, and BOM import from CSV. Use this for any part where you are buying sub-components and adding value through assembly.

Inputs Required
  • BOM lines: part number, description, unit cost (£), quantity per assembly
  • Assembly operations: operation name, machine/station type, cycle time (h), OEE, Manning
  • Sub-assembly costs (nested assemblies)
  • Annual volume and region
Step-by-Step Workflow
  1. Select Assembly BOM tab or ⊕ New Costing → Assemblies tile.
  2. Add BOM lines: enter each component with unit cost and quantity. Use Import CSV for large BOMs.
  3. Add assembly operations: torque tighten, press fit, clip, weld, test. Enter cycle time per operation.
  4. For sub-assemblies, enter the sub-assembly cost as a BOM line item.
  5. Set overhead %, margin %, and annual volume.
  6. Click Calculate. The model sums BOM cost + assembly labour + machine cost + overhead + margin.
Tips
  • For manual assembly stations, use Manning = 1.0 and set machine cost = 0 (labour-only station).
  • Robotic assembly: Manning = 0.1–0.25 but machine hourly rate is £45–120/hr vs. £18–35/hr manual.
  • CSV import saves time for 20+ component BOMs — download the template from the Import button.
Common Mistakes
  • Forgetting fasteners and consumables (adhesives, lubricants) — these are often 2–5% of total BOM cost.
  • Using supplier quoted sub-assembly costs without should-cost validation — always cost critical sub-assemblies independently.
  • Ignoring end-of-line test cost — functional test at 30–90 s per assembly adds £0.20–0.80/part at UK labour rates.
Overview — Blow Moulding

Blow moulding produces hollow thermoplastic parts (bottles, tanks, ducts). Three main variants: EBM (extrusion blow — parison extruded then blown), IBM (injection blow — preform injection-moulded then blown), and SBM/ISBM (stretch blow — PET preform biaxially oriented for bottles). EBM dominates automotive (fuel tanks, ducting); SBM/ISBM for PET bottles.

🆕 Latest features (2026): blow mould-cost estimator + blow DFM advisor; EBM / IBM / SBM process labelling; auto cool-factor from wall thickness; SBM two-stage preform modelling; additive / masterbatch adder; and filled material gaps.
Inputs Required
  • Material (HDPE, PP, PET-BG, PA6, ABS-EBM)
  • Part weight (kg) and flash weight (kg) — total shot weight is sum of both
  • Wall thickness (mm) — drives cooling time via cool time constant
  • Parison extrusion time (s), blow time (s), open/close time (s)
  • Machine type: EBM (continuous extrusion), IBM (injected preform), SBM (stretch blow)
  • Cavities, OEE, manning, mould cost and life
  • Optional: deflash operation (cycle time, machine, labour)
  • Optional: reject rate (0–1) — uplifts material and cycle time
Cost Drivers
  • Material: 45–70% of cost. HDPE is cheapest (~£1.20/kg), PA6 highest (~£3.80/kg).
  • Tooling: Aluminium blow moulds £8–60k (far cheaper than injection moulds); life 500k–3M cycles.
  • Cycle time: Dominated by cooling. Thin-wall HDPE (2mm): ~12s. Thick-wall PA6 (5mm): ~80s.
  • Flash: EBM generates 10–30% flash (pinch-off scrap). Reground and re-used in most plants.
Overview — Thermoforming

Thermoforming heats a thermoplastic sheet until pliable, then forms it over a tool using vacuum, pressure, or matched dies. It produces large shallow panels (instrument panels, trunk liners, door skins) at lower tooling cost than injection moulding. Sheet can be single-material or multi-layer co-extruded.

Inputs Required
  • Sheet material grade (filtered to formable sheet — HIPS / ABS / PETG / APET / CPET / rigid-PVC / PP / PE / PMMA / PC / PEI / PPS / co-ex multilayer) and sheet thickness (mm)
  • Sheet weight (gross, heated in full) and part weight (net after trim); parts per sheet
  • Method: vacuum / pressure / twin-sheet — now drives forming energy and tool cost
  • Cycle: heat / form / cool / trim / index (s) — leave heat/cool at 0 to auto-estimate from thickness & material
  • Electricity £/kWh, reject %, optional additive / masterbatch (UV, anti-static, FR, impact) + let-down %
  • Thermoformer (small / large / pressure / twin-sheet / roll-fed / oven), tool cost (0 = auto-estimate) and amortisation volume
  • Tooling estimator: projected area, mould material (epoxy / cast-Al / CNC-Al / steel), complexity, trim type
  • Optional geometry for DFM & wall-thinning: draw depth, min opening, unsupported span, internal radius, draft angle, tolerance, plug assist / undercut / textured
Cost Drivers & Process Physics
  • Oven energy: part-level, from material cp·ΔT ÷ oven efficiency (0.18–0.28 kWh/kg of sheet) — thickness- and material-aware, not a flat machine lump. PC/PEI/PP cost more energy than HIPS.
  • Heating & cooling: auto heat time ∝ thickness1.6; auto cool time ∝ thickness² (water-cooled tool cools ~2× faster than ambient). Cooling is a real cycle element — previously omitted.
  • Sag: self-weight plate deflection ∝ span⁴ ÷ t² — low-melt-strength PP/PE/TPO on wide thin sheets flag high sag (needs plug assist / sag bands).
  • Material utilisation: skeleton/trim scrap 20–50% of sheet; the trimmed skeleton is credited back at the grade's regrind (scrap-recovery) value.
  • Tooling: parametric estimate — mould (epoxy £1.5k → steel £15k+ base) × area × complexity × method × cavities, with expected life (5k → 3M cycles) and £/cycle maintenance. Pressure/twin-sheet tools cost 1.6×/2.2× vacuum.
  • Draw ratio → wall thinning: predicts average & corner wall from depth ÷ opening; flags over-draw beyond the method limit (vacuum ~1.5:1, +plug ~3:1, pressure ~4:1) → webbing / thin walls.
  • Method: vacuum → pressure (+~6%) → twin-sheet (+~14%) on a typical panel, driven by forming energy + tooling.
DFM Advisor & Country Pricing
  • Thermoforming DFM panel scores geometry (draw ratio, wall thinning, sag, internal radius, draft angle, undercut, texture wash-out, tolerance realism, formability) and lists critical/major/minor issues live as you edit.
  • Authentic country pricing: per-country £/kg for 18 sheet grades × US/DE/PL/CN/IN/MX/TH/VN — real regional quotes, not a UK×factor. Commodity sheet swings ±~20%; specialities near-flat.
  • Supplier tiers: overhead/margin now split Tier-1 / Tier-2 / Tier-3 (automotive validation vs lean packaging).
🆕 Latest features (2026): full process-physics engine — material + thickness heating time and part-level oven energy (cp·ΔT), sag prediction, cooling model, and method-driven forming energy/tooling (vacuum / pressure / twin-sheet, previously cosmetic); parametric mould + trim estimator with mould-material life & maintenance; draw-ratio → wall-thinning predictor; thermoforming DFM advisor; 18 dedicated sheet grades (commodity / engineering / high-perf / multilayer co-ex) with authentic per-country pricing; +5 machines (pressure / twin-sheet / roll-fed / oven / CNC trim router); dedicated thermoforming & trim-router labour; Tier-1/2/3 overhead; and additive / masterbatch adders.
Accuracy note: the model structure is engineering-correct; reference prices and physics coefficients are benchmark-anchored (confidence: Low) — ready to be calibrated against live sheet-price feeds and measured cycle times.
Overview — Rotational Moulding

Rotational moulding (rotomoulding) is a low-pressure process for large, hollow, seamless parts. Powdered resin is loaded into a mould mounted on a rotating arm, heated in an oven until the powder fuses to the mould walls, then cooled and demoulded. Ideal for tanks, playground equipment, kayaks, and large automotive components. Typical part weight 0.5–30 kg.

🆕 Latest features (2026): rotomoulding material family & gaps; roto machine ladder; cycle-time predictor (oven + cool) + mould-cost estimator + roto DFM advisor; and oven fuel energy costed.
Inputs Required
  • Material (LLDPE powder is most common; HDPE, nylon, PP also available)
  • Part weight (kg) and powder adder (£/kg grinding premium over pellet price)
  • Number of arms on carousel (typically 2–4) and parts per arm
  • Heating time (s), cooling time (s), load/unload time (s)
  • Machine (carousel rotomoulder), OEE, manning
  • Mould cost and expected life (typically 3–10k cycles for aluminium moulds)
Cost Drivers
  • Cycle time: Very long (30–90 min) due to oven and cooling. Multiple arms partially offset this — one arm loads/unloads while others heat/cool.
  • Material: LLDPE powder costs ~30–40% more per kg than injection pellets (grinding cost). This is captured by the "Powder Adder" field.
  • Tooling: Cast aluminium moulds are relatively cheap (£2–15k) but have shorter life than steel injection tools.
Overview — Rubber Moulding

Rubber moulding covers compression moulding, transfer moulding, and injection moulding of elastomers (NR, SBR, EPDM, NBR, silicone). Key automotive applications: seals, gaskets, grommets, vibration mounts, and hoses. Cycle includes a cure (vulcanisation) step that is time and temperature sensitive.

🆕 Latest features (2026): cure-time (t90) kinetics predictor; rubber mould-cost estimator; rubber DFM advisor; expanded elastomer grades; deflash + inspection operations; and family-aware regional compound pricing.
Inputs Required
  • Compound type and part weight (g or kg)
  • Process type: compression, transfer, or injection
  • Cure temperature (°C) and cure time (min)
  • Cavities per mould, mould cost and life
  • Optional: post-cure, trimming (deflashing), secondary operations
Cost Drivers
  • Cure time: Unlike thermoplastics (cool to eject), rubber must chemically cure. Typical cure time 2–15 min at 150–190°C.
  • Flash: Compression and transfer moulding produce flash that requires manual or automated trimming — add 0.05–0.15 hr/part for deflash.
  • Material: EPDM 30–40% of cost at £1.80–3.50/kg; silicone is 3–5× more expensive than EPDM.
Overview — Composites (CFRP / GFRP)

Composites combine reinforcing fibres (carbon or glass) with a resin matrix (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester). Processes range from manual hand layup to automated AFP/ATL. Key automotive applications: CFRP structural components (floor tunnels, crash structures, body panels), GFRP body panels, and continuous fibre composite spring leaves.

Inputs Required
  • Fibre type (carbon/glass), form (prepreg/dry), and price (£/kg)
  • Resin type and price if dry fibre (epoxy, vinyl ester)
  • Part weight (kg), fibre volume fraction (Vf), fibre/matrix weight ratio
  • Layup labour and time (hr/part) — depends on ply count and geometry
  • Cure machine (autoclave, press, oven, RTM press) and cure cycle time
  • Trim machine (5-axis router, waterjet) and trim time
  • Tooling cost and life (INVAR for autoclave, aluminium for press)
Cost Drivers
  • Material: 40–65% of cost. Carbon fibre prepreg: £28–80/kg. Dry CF: £20–35/kg. E-glass: £3–6/kg.
  • Labour: Hand layup is 60–80% of non-material cost. AFP/ATL reduces labour but requires £250–350/hr cell rate.
  • Cure cycle: Autoclave curing: £35–80/hr but adds 4–8 hr/cycle. Resin transfer moulding (RTM) cures in 10–30 min but needs matched metal tools.
  • Scrap: High — unacceptable porosity or delamination. Budget 5–15% reject rate.
Overview — Extrusion

Extrusion forces molten thermoplastic through a shaped die to produce continuous profiles (tubes, channels, seals, weatherstripping). The profile is then cut to length, cooled, and optionally co-extruded with a second material. Key automotive applications: window seals (EPDM co-extruded TPE/EPDM), fluid tubes (PA/PE), structural profiles.

🆕 Latest features (2026): process-physics line-rate estimator (screw-output vs cooling-limited haul-off — thick-wall pipe is now correctly cooling-limited); per-material specific-energy (kWh/kg) variable-energy model; die-swell reference; a parametric die + calibration/sizing tooling estimator (pipe / profile / sheet / cable / medical-tube / co-ex by size & layers); a full scrap chain (startup + steady-state + colour/die-change purge); additive / masterbatch let-down adder; downstream cut-off/QA and optional pressure/leak-test operations; an extrusion DFM advisor (wall min/uniformity, corners, tolerance realism, hollow-chamber & co-ex-layer checks); a process-line machine ladder (pipe / profile / sheet / cable / co-ex / micro-tube) and an extrusion-grade material ladder (PE100/PE80 pipe, uPVC pipe, PVC/XLPE cable, PS/ABS/PMMA/PC sheet, medical PVC/TPU tube, TPE profile, PVC foam, PA12) plus a commodity-filtered material picker.
Inputs Required
  • Material grade and linear density (kg/m) — determines material cost per metre
  • Part length (m) and cut-to-length operation
  • Extrusion speed (m/min) and line speed
  • Die cost and life (typically 3–10M metres)
  • Optional: co-extrusion (second material weight and price), downstream punching/notching
Cost Drivers
  • Material: Typically 50–70% of cost. EPDM seals: £2.50–4.50/kg. PA12 tube: £4.00–6.00/kg.
  • Line rate: Extrusion lines run at £35–75/hr all-in. High-speed lines (>10 m/min) dramatically reduce per-part cost at volume.
  • Die tooling: Extrusion dies £2–20k, co-extrusion dies £8–40k. Life is very long (millions of metres) so amortisation is low.
All systems operational
